speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Mila Bachner. It's July 25, 1995. Mila, you would just telling me about how they were evacuating the camp, and that you knew at that time that the Russians were near.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got together. We were 900 girls our camp. 900. Some of them had already died or were sent away. And we started out on our journey. We called-- they called it the Todesmarsch, the death march. I had my coat from my sister. I had the-- I had the clogs that we wore, you know, the backless shoes. That-- that-- like the clogs, you know that one style. And it was January and the snow was high. The winter was very bitter. We could not take anything with us. We had actually nothing to take from-- from the camp, you know. We had no food. We had no water. And we started out just what we had on us. We walked for about January, February-- January, February, March, about two and a half months. About two and a half months this death march took. We walked day, and we walked night in hunger, in pain, in death. And just you name it, we had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept, during that time, if there was a Bauer, a farmer that would have a little heart, with the permission of the Gestapo, if he would say it's OK to let them in for a night, they would let us in. And they would also boil-- they had those big things for potatoes, those big pots. They would boil some potatoes for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, we had no food, we had no water, we had no toilet facilities, we had no baths. Did you ever go for two and a half months without a bath? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine what's going on in Bosnia and in all these places, in-- in-- in Africa, how people live? This is how it was with us. And, of course, people got sick. They died of-- they-- they-- they died of starvation. They got sick from starvation. Their feet were frozen off. Their hands were frozen off. The-- the-- We had no toilet facilities. We slept in this-- in this barn, this one farmer allowed us to stay in this barn one night. And you know, I slept-- there were horses and cows and pigs. It was a big barn. They kept everything very clean, you know, even with that. And I slept between a horse's legs. I just lied down there for safety's sake.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know something, [INAUDIBLE], and I mean this with all-- in all honesty, I was not afraid of the four-legged animals that they will kill me, I was afraid of the two-legged beasts, because they did the killing. I felt very safe, and I wished I could stay there forever with them if need be. You know? And if you didn't find a barn, where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. We just walked. We just walked along in hunger and in pain. Just like I told you, our faces were-- I remember-- I remember there was a second Bauer, a second farmer who allowed us a night over in the barn. And, I don't know, I went out of this-- I just went out of the barn to just look around, you know, I see this-- this German soldier. It seems that they were in the reserve in the-- in the barn-- in the back they had their barracks. And they saw girls. They must've seen the transport of girls coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so emaciated, so sick and at that point the lice had invaded our bodies, you know, and we-- we already got sick people. People fell to the-- to the-- to the floor, to the ground, and if they didn't die they would be shot. And if they didn't-- they weren't shot to death, they would bleed to death. We couldn't go help them because they will shoot us on top of them. So who wanted to die such a death and be left someplace in Germany? Nobody would ever know what happened to me. And how many times I have begged them already, why don't you just kill these people? If you-- if you shoot them, kill them. I mean, I don't know-- I spoken fluent German. I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. If you would kill a dog half dead, wouldn't you have pity on the dog and kill him? He says, but you are not a dog. And you weren't afraid to speak to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I wasn't-- they couldn't do more than kill me. They couldn't do more than kill me. I didn't care anymore. I was tired. I was hungry. [SOBBING] I was emotionally finished. I just existed. I just-- I don't know for what. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He didn't kill me, and he didn't kill them. They died. They died in pain in the-- the gutter. And then we stopped, and this-- and this-- and this soldier came out, you know. And he's holding the soldier bread in is hand, you know-- you know, the squares, the army bread. And he says to me-- you know, I-- I-- I had my black pigtails. I was emaciated. I was filthy. I had body lice already in my body. And I-- you know, my whole body was infested with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Do you-- are you hungry? And I said, yeah. He said, would you like to have this bread? [SPEAKING GERMAN]? [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I said, this would be very nice. Thank you. So he says to me, I'll give you the bread if you go to sleep with me in the scheune, if I go to sleep with him in the barn. You know, [INAUDIBLE], coming from the home such as I, with such a strong sense of morality, just like I spoke at the table, you know, and I said to myself, what is one to do? And if he's going to take me and rape me-- I had no idea it would rape was. I was so totally ignorant and so stupid on that subject. When did I ever learn about sex at home? How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could not even bring the word 'sex' into your mouth in front of a mommy and a daddy. We learned in-- Bais Yaakov, in Hebrew school. Our teacher, this was taught to us in the proper way. You couldn't come in [INAUDIBLE] having sex. This will be-- oh God, I wouldn't even dare.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You would like to sleep with a filthy pig Jew? I said to him. Isn't it enough that you slaughtering my family. [SPEAKING GERMAN] I said to him, my whole family is dead from your hands, I said, slaughtered. [SPEAKING GERMAN] I said now you want me, a filthy pig that has lice on her body and that is sick, sleep with me? I said, you try to touch me, I said, and you are dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, [INAUDIBLE], I don't know if you ever encountered the-- the [SPEAKING HEBREW], God's-- God's strength that he sends a certain strength into you. When-- when you believe in something very strongly, it will happen. I encountered something that I-- I am eternally grateful to God for giving me this power. Because I felt his power in my hand. And I think that I could've ripped this German apart if he as much as laid a finger on me. That's how powerful I felt beneath all the illness and all that hunger. But you know that I'm thinking, when he saw the lice, maybe it's the lice, they said, that saves me. I-- but my power was there anyway. You know, my power was there. You know, he got scared of me. He-- he left the bread and he went away, and he gave me this-- this soldier bread. It's the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know how long I did not tell this? You know why I didn't tell it? Because I was afraid of how people will conceive this story. Because they would say, how could you say that you would kill a German? I said, if I tried I could have killed him, I said, but maybe the lice scared him away, which is quite possible he didn't want the contract this illness, whatever I had. I had typhus, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- then we went to Pordizem. It was a beautiful town. The Germans have very clean people-- they should only, you know, I don't know, but they are very spotless people. And we are walking in this town. We are schlepping our feet. We already lost quite a few girls to the-- to the deaths and to the-- to the hunger, to the pain. And we hardly walked-- we could hardly walk on our feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we to come closer, we see a line. You know like a line in the street. You know that is-- that-- And in front of them, you-- we saw as we came closer and closer, objects like killing objects. You know, you could see rocks, you could see-- you could bottles, you could see clubs. And I said to myself, I said what-- what-- what is this? Wasn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: And as soon as we approach them, within enough distance, all these deadly objects started to fly at us. And I look at those young kids-- they were no more than 13, 14 years old, 15, with the yellow shorts, with the-- with the yellow suits with their red little things and the [? boat-- ?] It was the Hitler Youth. This is what they were trying to do. And the blood started to cover the faces. We had to duck. We didn't know where to hide. Our bodies were so fragile, so thin, so sick, and the clothes was not heavy, so the bottles penetrated the-- the glass. We were cut, we were--

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know something? With all this, I survived. You know, we went through this thing so fast, and I thought to myself, this is the civilized Germany. My uncles lived in Germany. My mother brought us there, my father had his brother there. I had family, cousins. And I said this is this culture that they-- they-- The civilized country with the knowledge and-- and-- and with everything and this is what they use the knowledge for, for mass murder of human beings? So here you are. We are teaching education to children. I try to educate them that with knowledge, with love and with knowledge, we can overcome. So we must be able to use the knowledge for the good of humanity. That's something that children must learn, people must learn. Being educated does not mean that one-- one that does the right thing with it. We have to learn that, too, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, all these things healed up. They did not kill us. They wounded us very badly, so they had dirty streets with blood. They had to clean the streets from the blood, the Germans, that's all, because we couldn't do it. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we finally came to a town called Floss. A little town called Floss-- F-L-O-S-S. They put us on little trucks through the forest to the camp in Flossenburg. OK? Floss and Flossenburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on the last truck with a friend of mine, you know, and we-- when all the trucks started to move already I and [? Shonda ?] [? Goldman, ?] who lives in Canada. But she lost a-- a-- a son or her husband, and she lost a little, this, you know. And we jumped off that truck. So for one night, we were free-- a night and a day. And we are walking around, you know, sick and filthy and with the lice, afraid to-- anybody should see us. They should not recognize us, you know, because we did not look like humans. We really looked like something from the other world, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, You know, [? Shonda, ?] we have to do one of two things. First of all, we are-- we are starved. We have to get some food. So would do you go get food? So as we walk there, we see the cemetery in Floss. I saw to cemetery when I was there, now, too, but I didn't have time to go take a picture, and I cried. So I said, you know, let's go in the cemetery, and she and I went on the cemetery. And we started to cry, and we started to scream of the [SPEAKING HEBREW], you know, on the graves of these people because I saw the [SPEAKING HEBREW], the names, the letters in Hebrew were falling off the graves and laying there. So I picked up a daled for David to finish the David. And I picked up-- and we cried. We lay there. And we dig into the ground with our fingers, and we cry. And I said, please, dear God, open up your ears. Just listen to us. [SOBBING] Just for a little while, just listen to us and help us. Is that so much to ask? I'm talking to him. Nothing happened. His eyes were stuffed. His ears were stuffed. The [SPEAKING HEBREW] stuffed. Nothing worked, nothing. He turns his head totally. Pick ourselves up, and I said, you know, we have to get some food. So food you can get only in a-- in a house. So I said when we see the first-- the first house, I'll ring the bell. I speak German. I'll-- so she says, so what will you say? She was from a town where they didn't speak any German at all. So I said to her, listen, whatever will happen, you keep your mouth shut. You try to steal a little food if you can. I will do the talking. So what will you tell them? I'll tell them we are from Volksdeutsche-- you know-- you know-- you know what Volksdeutsche is? Like-- like the Polacks put on German uniforms, collaborators with the Germans, OK? This is a Volksdeutsche. Not a born German, but a [SPEAKING HEBREW]. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knock at the door and a beautiful lady opens up the door. White with the white apron. I remember so well. You know, and hair neatly combed. And I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said we-- we are hungry. So she looked at us, and she sees the-- the things, the lice. She wouldn't let us in. And then she says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? How can I leave you alone? I-- I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So, you know, she took us in, and she gave us food, but she would not let us sit at the table. You know? And she gave us underwear to change. And we left the other underwear there so she had a few lice because everything was [INAUDIBLE]. And she asked if we want to sleep there, and I was afraid because she started to talk politic-- how did it happen? I-- she said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], that the Russians are close. I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I don't understand any of this. And you know something? I didn't. I was not a politician. I was a-- I was a slave child. And we left the house at about 12 o'clock and we said goodnight. She gave-- we took some food. She stole some food, the other one she went into the toilet. So she already looked around a little bit, and we went out, and we went back to the cemetery to sit in peace and eat up our food. OK? And the-- and then it was like maybe 6 o'clock in the morning. I said, [? Shonda, ?] what are we going to do? So I said I want to go back to camp, wherever it is. I don't want to be alone here. If they will find us, they will kill us, and then we will be lost somewhere and I-- I don't know where. So she says, you know, [SPEAKING POLISH]. They said in Poland, you know, back-- a goat goes backwards. But I said, in my case, we are not goats, I said. I-- I'm not-- I don't want to die here like a dog in someplace, you know. And so we start at the very same forest that I went with the bus. I just went there in April of 1923-- 1923, April. And-- April 23rd, '95. Oy vey is me. I'm so famished at this point already. And we meet a guy on a bicycle. The forest is not the same because they already built homes there. They removed-- but the road is there. And he said-- and I ask them both, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Where is the camp that the haftling girl came yesterday? And he told me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Who are you? And we told him that we fell off the truck. The truck was crowded and that we fell off and [GERMAN], we are going there. So he came-- he told us where to go. Now, the pictures, the little pictures that you have there, I'd like to explain it. I had this on my neck, OK? I had the pictures on my neck that I took from home when the Nazis came in. I didn't run for the gold, which we didn't have anyway, I ran for pictures. I thought, whatever happens, let me have pictures of my family. And I made myself a little sack myself on a rope and I had this on my neck all through the six years, OK? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah. This is me in 1939. It was taken by the Nazis because I needed this for my ID for the camp card, for my ID from the Nazis. This is my little sister. This is the original picture, my little sister, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mesia. She was 10 years old. She was a beautiful child. She went to Auschwitz with my brother and my daddy. This is my brother Marvi, who survived the war. My beautiful brother. And this is my sister Malka, her wedding and family.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll look at this pictures later when we can look a little close-up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Flossenburg-- after I left the cemetery, we started walking. That guy told us where to go, and we-- we came to Flossenburg, and we walk in to a gate. At the gate, this Gestapo meets us, and he said, [GERMAN]? And we told them exactly what happened, how we fell off the truck, and we got hurt, I said. But we came here because I am a haftling. I am a slave, and I want to be with my-- with my friends. They were already up on the barrack, up on the hill, you know, the other people. And us he told to take off our clothes, strip ourselves to nakedness. So he saw the bodies with the lice. We look [YIDDISH]. They should only look like that, OK, one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And both of us are standing, and then he sees these pictures on my neck. And he says to me, [GERMAN]? I-- what have you got there? And I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], So he says to me-- he-- I told him those are pictures of my family from home. And he said, you are not going to need these anymore. I said, [GERMAN]. As long as I live, I said, I'm going to need them, I said. My family, I said, is already slaughtered. [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, [GERMAN], I am still alive, and this is-- this is going to keep me alive. And as long as I live, I want them. So he became very-- because I said no and he said yes, and I disobeyed, he started to pull-- it was a heavy rope, like-- like a-- like a rope, you know, and he started to pull, so I'm holding this rope like this, you know, not to ruin the pictures. And I'm resisting with all of my might, because he could cut into my neck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he couldn't do anything, so he takes off the-- his-- his rifle and he gives me a knock and I fall to the floor. I was bleeding profusely, and if I'm not mistaken-- he inflicted this on me. This was opened up. OK? But I can't, there is nothing-- I-- I didn't want to do anything. I wanted to have this as my souvenir of what had happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he left. He thought I was dead because, you know, he gave me a split, and I-- I just fell. I found myself on top of the barrack with the other girls. I had all dried-out blood on me. I was all bloody and I had-- and-- and I felt for my pictures, and I had them on my neck. And I said, thank God. I said thank you, God, in Jewish, for this gift. Then they-- that-- that same day they gave us clothes, you know, the dresses already from dead people with the-- with the letter KL, konzentrationslager. Big sizes. You know, they-- they-- this was an exterminating camp also, Flossenburg, where the crematoriums were there, you'll see it on the picture. And after 12 days of suffering in this camp, what I went through, what I saw, again, there was an-- an order given that we must meet tomorrow morning-- after-- this was after 10-- after 12 days, I think. We have to meet in the front on the-- by the tracks, in front of the train. And we got down and there's a cattle wagon-- cattle wagons are waiting for us. We already were out-- from the 900 girls maybe 500 were left. I-- I'm not sure. You know, they lost-- we lost the lives there. And then we were put on those cattle trains for eight days. [INAUDIBLE], if anybody is going to tell you this is impossible that it happened, don't ever let them say it. I lived it. I was on it, and I'm here to tell you that. Eight days in a closed cattle wagon without air, food, toilet, water. Nothing. Nothing. We laid there like-- like dead animals, but the heart was going. Twice on the eight-day trip, it arrived from plus Flossenburg to Bergen-Belsen, OK. They opened the doors and the we had to throw the dead bodies out onto tracks because people died in that-- in the-- in the wagon and the car, and we had to throw them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one time this Gestapo standing by the door, and he's drinking from the-- you know, water from his-- what do you call this-- this-- the soldiers use? The canteen. From his canteen. And-- and I said to him, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Can I have a little water? And he said, [GERMAN]. And I said, danke schoen. So he takes and spills it out right in front of me and he closes the door. Just an example of humanity. OK? And we are the inhumans, they are the humane people. We arrived to Bergen-Belsen, and I saw carts waiting for us but there were no horses. There were human horses. Those were skeletons from Bergen-Belsen already. You know? And we are supposed to go on those carts and they are supposed to take us to the hospitals. When I saw this, I knew that these people were going to die-- to their deaths, you know. I got off the train. My feet were like sticks, my legs like two sticks, wobbling like a new-born lamb. OK? I hardly held on to my own life. I was sick. I had a-- a full-blown typhus. I had hundred-and-six fever. I was emaciated totally. Ok? And I said to myself, no way am I going on that thing. I said to myself, [? [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. ?] I spoke Jewish to myself. I got off that thing and I walked slowly with whoever else walked with me, and I hoped that I'm not going to be noticed by the Gestapo or the SS or the Wehrmacht, whatever, how I walk because they would easily throw me on the fires there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made it to the barrack. [SIGHS] The wall of my barrack was situated over there, for instance. This was this direction. When I walked in, the floor was full of dead bodies. Full with dead bodies from dysentery, from typhus, from hair loss. You name it, and they had it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lice, first all, caused the biggest problem, because my skin was already yellow with the pus, everything it was. I had-- I had nothing. I had nothing on me except my heart was going. Did you ever see a board? That's what I was. My heart was going. I had a-- I had a good heart, evidently.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there for two weeks. We were a slave labor camp there, and our main job was to take the dead bodies onto tracks, out to the fires. So can you imagine when they come in, you know, those people when they come to take the bodies and if I would be laying, not moving, they would take me out, too. So-- and if I was alive, then I had to help take bodies out. So I pick myself, you know, up with all my might and I took the two feet from the-- from those dead skeletons, and I would schlep them like this. And the fire was like a little bit further out, there was a fire burning, gasoline fire for the bodies, gasoline fire with bodies. And this was like a constant-- like a torch, constantly burning. Then I walked back to the barrack, and I laid down. But finally, I had hundred-and-six marked fever, so Mitzi Maylor, who was the [GERMAN] in my camp, said-- came over to me with a doctor, a woman doctor from Czechoslovakia, and she said, do me a favor. Check this child. She wanted me to go to the kitchen to work. And I said, Miss Maylor, I cannot do it. She was Jewish. You know. And this doctor came and she said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], two days to live. And I hear this, you know, my mind was OK, and I said, you tell her that she is going to live two days, and I'm going to make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my friend Sally did work in the kitchen, and she would come to me with food, the bread which had the glass ground in. People died from that anyway. I didn't touch it. But I said, Sally, [SPEAKING POLISH], I said the Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH], Hot-- hot-- you got hot coffee. And she would bring me the hot coffee, and I would put this coffee into my mouth as if it was a piece of ice. And I felt that with this heat somehow I killed the fever in my inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: If this is at all possible, then this is what happened to me. I don't care. That's how I came through it. My fever went down. To-- and then we hear that the British invaded, that the British liberated Bergen-Belsen.
